**Nildo Silva**[**Federal**JuniorUniversity|***[nildo.silva@ufba.br](mailto:nildo.silva@ufba.br)***of]Bahia **Luana Martins**[**Federal University**|***[martins.luana@ufba.br](mailto:martins.luana@ufba.br)***of Bahia] **Larissa** Rocha**Federal University of Bahia**|***[lrsoares@uefs.br](mailto:lrsoares@uefs.br)***/ State]Univ. of Feira de Santana **Heitor**Costa[**Federal University**|***[heitor@ufla.br](mailto:heitor@ufla.br)***of Lavras **Ivan Machado**[**Federal University**|***[ivan.machado@ufba.br](mailto:ivan.machado@ufba.br)***of Bahia]

**Abstract**Developing test code may be a time­consuming process that requires much effort and cost, especially when done manually. In addition, during this process, developers and testers are likely to adopt bad design choices, which may lead to introducing the so­called test smells in the test code. As the test code with test tests might become more complex, and as a consequence, much more challenging to understand and evolve them correctly.Therefore, test smells may harm the test code quality and maintenance and break the whole software testing activities. In this context, this study aims to understand whether software testing practitioners unintentionally insert test smells when they implement test code. We first carried out an expert survey to analyze the usage frequency of a set of test smells and then interviews to reach a deeper understanding of how practitioners deal with test smells. Sixty professionals participated in the survey, and fiftyin the interviews. The yielded results indicate that experienced professionals introduce test smells during their daily programming tasks, even when using their companies’ standardized practices. Additionally, tools support test development and quality improvement, but most interviewees are not aware of test smells’ concepts.

***Keywords:****Test**Smells, Survey Study, Interview**Mixed­Method Research.*

**4.2 Results**

Wereceived from three (66.7%), 19 respondents from São Paulo (31.7%), and one re­ spondent from Paraná (1.6%). The respondents ranged from 22 to ance ranged ence as software developers also ranged from 0 to 13 years (average 1.67). Regarding gender, 35 respondents were male (65%), 19 dents were non­binary (3%). Most of Science­related courses (50 respondents ­ 83.3%), six respon­ dents (10%) nology, spondents (6.7%) hold a degree in other areas. Most of the re­ spondents (54 respondents ­ 90%) pursued higher education degrees, as follows: 40 respondents hold a bachelor’s degree (66.7%), 13 respondents hold a graduate degree (21.7%), and one respondent holds a postdoc (1.6%). Regarding the software testing tasks they commonly per­ form, (i) at the with more frequency than create (21.7%); and (iii) 8 respon­ dents create tests with more frequency than execute (13.3%). Moreover, 12 respondents only execute test cases (20%); one respondent only creates test cases (1.7%). ### 5 Interview

After carrying out the survey study, we interviewed Software Engineers to gather further evidence on how the practitioners deal with test smells dataset, including the interview transcriptions, interviewees profile, and coding summary, ,).et2021

The interviewer and interviewees did not access any test code from interviewees to analyze the presence of test smells. At the swered a ground and professional experience. They also provided an email to data analysis. We interviewed on June 3rd and June 30th. Due to the pandemic period, online meeting tools, such as Skype and Google Meet, were used upon the participants’ request. Werecorded the interviews with either the Skype conversa­ tion recording tool or the Google Meet screen capture feature. Additionally, terview.
